# Passage:  
Quadruplexes are a form of DNA secondary structure that can occur in G-rich or C-rich DNA sequences and are composed of either four individual single strands of DNA or are the result of internal folding of one single strand of DNA. C-rich i-motifs (iMs) are formed when unprotonated and protonated C–C+ base pairs intercalate and stabilize the secondary structure.  
In mammalian cells, iMs have been shown to be subject to two epigenetic modifications: methylation and hydroxymethylation. It is possible that the presence of 5-methylcytosine (5mC) or 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) within a C-rich DNA sequence can affect iM stability. In order to examine the effects of epigenetic modification on iM stability, researchers subjected three 22-nucleotide sequences to both pH and thermal denaturation. The wild-type (WT) sequence was 5′–TTCCCTACCCTCCCCACCCTAA–3′, and the modified sequences had either a 5mC (5mC-WT) or a 5hmC (5hmC-WT) at position five.  
The pH-dependent unfolding of each oligonucleotide solution was monitored using circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. The CD signal was monitored at 289 nm, and the concentration of DNA was 3 μM. The results are shown in Figure 1.  
CP-9-1-202CP_84573-00P1.gif  
Figure 1The pH-dependence of iM denaturation for each oligonucleotide
The temperature-dependence of iM denaturation was also monitored at 289 nm. The midpoint of denaturation of WT, 5mC-WT, and 5hmC-WT were 42.5ºC, 43.7ºC, and 40.5ºC, respectively.
